name: Hana Center
description_short: HUD-certified non-profit providing free housing counseling, public benefits assistance, and immigration services to Korean, Asian American, and immigrant communities in Chicagoland.
description_long:
HANA Center is a Chicago-based non-profit organization founded to build power within Korean, Asian American, and multiethnic immigrant communities through social services, education, culture, and community organizing. The organization's name reflects its mission—in Korean, HANA means 'one'—and it serves low- to medium-income households across the Chicagoland region. HANA Center operates with a human rights framework, addressing systemic barriers that immigrant and communities of color face in accessing housing, employment, legal status, and public benefits.

The organization offers a comprehensive range of free services including HUD-certified housing counseling, public benefits navigation (particularly SNAP), senior services, immigration and legal services, youth empowerment programs, and workforce development. Housing services are a core offering, with certified counselors providing no-cost one-on-one counseling, workshops, and referrals. Services span pre-purchase homebuyer counseling, post-purchase homeowner counseling, foreclosure prevention, rental assistance, eviction prevention, and homeless counseling. HANA also assists with Illinois Court-Based Rental Assistance Program (CBRAP) applications for tenants facing eviction. All counseling is available in Korean, Spanish, and English, with multilingual support extending to 15+ Asian American and Pacific Islander languages.

What distinguishes HANA Center is its deep cultural competency and multilingual capacity combined with its community organizing mission. Unlike transactional financial service providers, HANA integrates social services with civic engagement and advocacy. The organization coordinates with government agencies (Illinois Housing Development Authority, City of Chicago), serves as a HUD-approved housing counselor hub, and partners with national coalitions on fair housing initiatives. Their approach addresses both immediate financial crises and longer-term community empowerment, treating clients as partners in systemic change rather than solely as consumers of services.

HANA Center is genuinely free and operates as a registered non-profit, making it ideal for underserved populations with limited resources. The primary caveat is geographic limitation—services are explicitly focused on Chicagoland, not available nationally. Additionally, while HANA provides application assistance for rental assistance programs, they do not make eligibility or approval decisions; those determinations rest with government agencies like IHDA. The organization's community organizing focus means some clients may be engaged in advocacy activities, which may or may not align with all users' preferences.
pros: HUD-certified housing counselors providing genuinely no-cost counseling and workshops
Multilingual support in Korean, Spanish, English, and 15+ Asian American and Pacific Islander languages
Covers full spectrum of housing needs: pre-purchase, post-purchase, foreclosure prevention, rental, eviction prevention, and homelessness
Direct assistance with Illinois Court-Based Rental Assistance Program (CBRAP) applications for tenants facing eviction
Free public benefits navigation, including SNAP rule change updates and senior services support
Integrated immigration and legal services referrals alongside financial counseling
Non-profit status with transparent mission focused on community empowerment, not profit extraction
cons: Geographic limitation: services restricted to Chicagoland area only, not available nationally
HANA does not make decisions on rental assistance applications—only provides application assistance; approvals determined by external agencies like IHDA
Limited information on wait times, appointment availability, or response times for housing counseling requests
Website does not clearly specify which senior services are free versus potentially fee-based
Community organizing and civic engagement components may involve advocacy that not all clients wish to participate in
best_for: Low- to medium-income renters facing eviction or needing rental assistance in Chicagoland
Korean American and Asian American immigrant families navigating housing and public benefits in Chicago area
First-time homebuyers and struggling homeowners seeking foreclosure prevention counseling in Illinois
Seniors and immigrant families needing multilingual support for public benefits (SNAP, housing) enrollment
diagnosis: HANA Center is best for low-income immigrant households and seniors in Chicagoland seeking free, culturally competent housing counseling, public benefits assistance, and financial literacy support. The main caveat is strict geographic limitation to the Chicago area and the understanding that application decisions for government assistance programs rest with external agencies, not HANA itself.
services: Post-purchase homeowner counseling (property tax exemptions, refinancing, home repair/maintenance)
Foreclosure prevention and loss mitigation assistance
Rental housing counseling and eviction prevention support
Illinois Court-Based Rental Assistance Program (CBRAP) application assistance
Workforce empowerment and job placement assistance
Community education workshops on fair housing and financial topics
typical_results_timeline: Counseling available within 1-2 weeks of contact.
